strands.name: Number & Place Value
strands.description: Reading, writing, comparing and rounding numbers.

teach.title: Place value
teach.plain: Place value tells you how much each digit is worth. The same digit means something different depending on where it sits.
rules: Reading right to left: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, ten thousands.
A 4 in the ones column is worth 4. A 4 in the hundreds column is worth 400.
Zeros are placeholders. They keep other digits in the right column.
teach.example: In 3,472 the 4 is in the hundreds column, so it means 400. The 3 is in the thousands column, so it means 3,000.
teach.tryThis: What is the value of the 7 in 8,741?

workedExample.question: Write the value of each digit in 5,283.
steps: Start at the right-hand digit: 3 is in the ones column, so it means 3.
Next is 8, in the tens column, so it means 80.
Next is 2, in the hundreds column, so it means 200.
Last is 5, in the thousands column, so it means 5,000.

practice.question: What is the value of the 6 in 4,632?
practice.workingOut: The 6 sits in the hundreds column, so it is worth 600.

practice.question: What is the value of the 9 in 9,214?
practice.workingOut: The 9 is in the thousands column, so it is worth 9,000.

practice.question: Which number has a 5 worth 50?
practice.workingOut: In 2,051 the 5 is in the tens column, so it means 50.

practice.question: In 7,408, what is the value of the 4?
practice.workingOut: The 4 sits in the hundreds column, so it is worth 400.

practice.question: What is the value of the 0 in 5,031?
practice.workingOut: The 0 is a placeholder in the hundreds column. It holds the column but is worth nothing.

teach.title: Comparing numbers
teach.plain: To compare two numbers, look at the digits from the left. The first digit that is different tells you which number is bigger.
rules: First, count the digits. More digits = bigger number.
If both numbers have the same number of digits, compare from the left.
Use < for 'less than' and > for 'greater than'. The open mouth points to the bigger number.
teach.example: Compare 4,523 and 4,498. Same digits (4). Next: 5 is bigger than 4. So 4,523 > 4,498.
teach.tryAnswer: 3,270 (hundreds digit 2 vs tens digit 0, the hundreds differ: 2 > 0)

workedExample.question: Put these numbers in order, smallest first: 2,341, 2,314, 2,431, 2,143
steps: Compare hundreds: 2,143 has 1; the other three have 3 or 4.
Of the others, 2,314 and 2,341 have hundreds digit 3; 2,431 has 4, so it is largest.

practice.question: Which symbol goes in the box? 4,523 □ 4,532
Which is the largest?
practice.workingOut: Hundreds digits: 4, 4, 9, 0. Largest hundreds is 9. So 3,904.

practice.question: Which is the smallest?
practice.workingOut: All start 1. Hundreds: 2, 3, 2, 4. Two start 1,2. Tens: 3 vs 4. 1,234 is smallest.

practice.question: Order smallest first: 567, 657, 576. Which is in the middle?
practice.workingOut: In order: 567, 576, 657. The middle one is 576.

practice.question: Which statement is true?
practice.workingOut: 1,100 is bigger because the hundreds digit is 1 vs 0.

teach.title: Rounding numbers
teach.plain: Rounding means making a number simpler. We round to the nearest 10, 100 or 1,000 so numbers are easier to work with.
rules: Find the digit to the RIGHT of the place you are rounding to.
If it is 5 or more, round UP.
If it is 4 or less, round DOWN.
All digits after the rounded place become zero.
teach.example: Round 347 to the nearest 100. Look at the tens digit (4). 4 is less than 5, so round DOWN to 300.

workedExample.question: Round 4,650 to the nearest 1,000.
steps: We are rounding to the nearest 1,000, so look at the hundreds digit.
6 is 5 or more, so round UP.
The thousands digit 4 becomes 5, and all digits after become zero.

practice.question: Round 67 to the nearest 10.
practice.workingOut: Ones digit is 7, which is 5 or more. Round up to 70.

practice.question: Round 432 to the nearest 100.
practice.workingOut: Tens digit is 3, less than 5. Round down to 400.

practice.question: Round 2,750 to the nearest 1,000.
practice.workingOut: Hundreds digit is 7, so round up to 3,000.

practice.question: Round 849 to the nearest 10.
practice.workingOut: Ones digit is 9, so round up to 850.

practice.question: Round 6,320 to the nearest 100.
practice.workingOut: Tens digit is 2, less than 5. Round down to 6,300.

practice.question: Round 9,499 to the nearest 1,000.
practice.workingOut: Hundreds digit is 4, less than 5. Round down to 9,000.

practice.question: Round 5,551 to the nearest 100.
practice.workingOut: For nearest 100, look at the tens digit. Tens digit is 5, so round up to 5,600.

teach.title: Negative numbers
teach.plain: Negative numbers are less than zero. We see them in temperatures, money owed and going below ground floor.
rules: Numbers get bigger going right on a number line.
-5 is SMALLER than -2 (further from zero).
To go from a negative to a positive, count up through zero.
The difference between two temperatures is how many steps between them.
teach.example: The temperature was 4°C, then dropped by 7°C. 4 - 7 = -3°C. Count back from 4 past zero: 4, 3, 2, 1, 0, -1, -2, -3.
teach.tryThis: The temperature is -2°C. It rises by 5°C. What is the new temperature?

workedExample.question: The temperature at midnight was 3°C. By 5am it had dropped by 8°C. What was the new temperature?
steps: We need to go DOWN by 8.
3 - 8 means go back 8 steps on the number line.
3 → 0 is 3 steps. We still need 5 more steps BELOW zero.

practice.question: 11°C drops by 17°C. What is the new temperature?
practice.workingOut: 11 - 17 = -6°C. Go back 11 to reach 0, then 6 more below zero.

practice.question: What is the difference between -3°C and 6°C?
practice.workingOut: From -3 to 0 is 3 steps. From 0 to 6 is 6 steps. Total: 9°C.

practice.question: Which is colder?
practice.workingOut: -7 is further below zero, so it is colder.

practice.question: Order from smallest: -4, 2, -1, 0, 3. Which is smallest?
practice.workingOut: -4 is furthest below zero, so it is smallest.

practice.question: The temperature is -5°C. It rises by 9°C. What is it now?
practice.workingOut: -5 + 9: go up 5 to 0, then 4 more to 4°C.

strands.name: Four Operations
strands.description: Adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing with bigger numbers.

teach.title: Column addition
teach.plain: Column addition lines up the digits under each other so you can add one column at a time.
rules: Line up the ones, tens, hundreds and thousands columns.
Start adding from the RIGHT (ones first).
If a column adds to 10 or more, carry the tens digit to the next column.
Write the carried digit small under the next column.
teach.example: 3,456 + 2,789. Ones: 6+9=15, write 5, carry 1. Tens: 5+8+1=14, write 4, carry 1. Hundreds: 4+7+1=12, write 2, carry 1. Thousands: 3+2+1=6. Answer: 6,245.

workedExample.question: Work out 15,678 + 9,432 using column addition.
steps: Line up: 15,678 above 9,432 (line up the ones).

practice.workingOut: Line up and add: ones 6+9=15 carry 1; tens 5+8+1=14 carry 1; hundreds 4+7+1=12 carry 1; thousands 3+2+1=6. Answer 6,245.

practice.question: Year 5 has 156 pupils and Year 6 has 143. How many altogether?
A library has 3,456 fiction and 2,789 non-fiction books. How many books in total?
A cinema sold 1,234 on Friday, 1,567 on Saturday and 1,098 on Sunday. Total tickets?

teach.title: Column subtraction
teach.plain: Column subtraction lines up the digits so you can take away one column at a time. When you can't take a bigger digit from a smaller one, you borrow from the next column.
rules: Line up the columns (ones under ones, tens under tens).
Start from the RIGHT (ones first).
If the top digit is smaller than the bottom, borrow 1 from the next column.
Borrowing from tens means the top ones digit gets 10 extra.
teach.example: 52 - 38. Ones: 2 - 8 can't do. Borrow 1 from tens: 12 - 8 = 4. Tens: 4 - 3 = 1. Answer: 14.

workedExample.question: Work out 8,234 - 3,567.
steps: Ones: 4 - 7 can't do. Borrow from tens. 3 becomes 2, ones becomes 14. 14 - 7 = 7.
Tens: 2 - 6 can't do. Borrow from hundreds. 2 becomes 1, tens becomes 12. 12 - 6 = 6.
Hundreds: 1 - 5 can't do. Borrow from thousands. 8 becomes 7, hundreds becomes 11. 11 - 5 = 6.

practice.workingOut: Borrow: ones 16-9=7; tens 4-8 borrow → 14-8=6; hundreds 0. Answer 67.

practice.question: A shop had 5,000 footballs. They sold 2,847. How many are left?
A farmer had 8,000 apples. He sold 3,245. How many left?
The museum had 1,567 visitors on Tuesday and 1,234 on Monday. How many MORE on Tuesday?
School needs £5,000. They have £2,750. How much more to raise?

teach.title: Times tables
teach.plain: Times tables are the building blocks of multiplication. Knowing them by heart makes every other bit of maths quicker.
rules: Multiplying is repeated adding. 4 × 3 means 4 + 4 + 4.
Order doesn't matter: 3 × 4 = 4 × 3.
To multiply by 10, add a zero to the end.
To multiply by 100, add two zeros.

workedExample.question: Work out 6 × 8 step by step.
steps: 6 × 8 is 'six lots of eight' OR 'eight lots of six' — same answer.

teach.title: Long multiplication
teach.plain: When a number is too big for your times tables, you can split it up, multiply each part, and add the answers together.
rules: Write the bigger number on top.
Multiply the top number by the ONES of the bottom.
Multiply the top number by the TENS of the bottom (remember to put a 0 in the ones place).
Add the two answers together.

workedExample.question: Work out 4,289 × 24.
steps: Step 2: Multiply 4,289 by 20 (the tens). First do 4,289 × 2 = 8,578, then add a zero: 85,780.

practice.question: A school orders 36 boxes of pencils with 24 in each. How many pencils?

teach.title: Division
teach.plain: Dividing means sharing equally or grouping. 20 ÷ 4 means 'how many fours fit into 20?'
rules: Division is the OPPOSITE of multiplication.
A remainder is what is left over after grouping.
If a question asks 'how many full buses/boxes?' you IGNORE the remainder. If it asks 'how many buses needed?' you ROUND UP.
teach.example: 20 ÷ 6. How many sixes in 20? Three sixes is 18. We have 2 left. Answer: 3 remainder 2.

workedExample.question: 216 children, max 20 per class. What is the minimum number of classes needed?
steps: Those 16 still need a class, so we need one MORE.

practice.question: 468 children split into 9 teams. How many per team?
216 cupcakes put into boxes of 27. How many boxes?
A bus holds 52. 320 passengers need a ride. How many buses?
practice.workingOut: 320 ÷ 52 = 6 remainder 8. 6 buses leave 8 people behind, so 7 buses needed.

practice.question: 8 kg of rice into 500g bags. How many bags?
Tom saves £2.50 each week. How many weeks to save £40?

teach.title: Factors & multiples
teach.plain: Factors are numbers that divide evenly into another number. Multiples are the answers you get from times tables.
rules: A factor of 12 is a number that divides into 12 with NO remainder.
The factors of 12 are: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12.
Multiples of 5 are: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25... (the 5 times table).
Every number is a factor of itself and has 1 as a factor.
teach.example: Factors of 10: 1, 2, 5, 10 (because 1×10=10 and 2×5=10). Multiples of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20...

workedExample.question: List all the factors of 18.
steps: Try 4: 18 ÷ 4 has a remainder, so 4 is not a factor.
Try 5: 18 ÷ 5 has a remainder, so 5 is not a factor.
6 already on the list, so we stop.

practice.question: Which of these is a factor of 24?
Which is NOT a multiple of 6?
practice.workingOut: 26 ÷ 6 = 4 remainder 2, so 26 is not a multiple of 6.

practice.question: What is the smallest number that is a multiple of both 3 and 4?
practice.workingOut: Multiples of 3: 3,6,9,12. Multiples of 4: 4,8,12. Smallest common one is 12.

practice.question: Which number has exactly 2 factors?
practice.workingOut: 7 has only 1 and 7 as factors. This makes it a prime number.

strands.name: Fractions, Decimals & Percentages
strands.description: Parts of a whole — three different ways of showing them.

teach.title: Fractions of amounts
teach.plain: A fraction tells you how many equal parts to split something into, and how many of those parts you want.
rules: The BOTTOM number (denominator) tells you how many equal parts.
The TOP number (numerator) tells you how many parts you want.
To find 3/5 of a number: divide by 5, then multiply by 3.
Divide first, then multiply. Bottom number first, top number second.

workedExample.question: What is 3/5 of 70?
steps: Divide the amount by the BOTTOM number: 70 ÷ 5 = 14.

teach.title: Equivalent fractions
teach.plain: Equivalent fractions are different ways of writing the same amount. 1/2 and 2/4 look different but mean the same.
rules: Multiply (or divide) the TOP and BOTTOM by the SAME number.
This makes a new fraction equal to the first.
To simplify: find the biggest number that divides into BOTH top and bottom.
Keep simplifying until no number (except 1) divides both.
teach.example: 6/8: divide top and bottom by 2 → 3/4. Cannot simplify further. So 6/8 = 3/4.

workedExample.question: Convert 5 and 6/8 into an improper fraction.
steps: An improper fraction has the top number bigger than the bottom.
Take the whole number (5) and multiply it by the denominator (8): 5 × 8 = 40.

practice.workingOut: Multiply top and bottom by 3: 3×3=9, 4×3=12. Answer 9.

teach.title: Decimals
teach.plain: Decimals are another way of writing fractions. The decimal point separates whole numbers from parts of a whole.
rules: First digit after the point = TENTHS (1/10).
Second digit after the point = HUNDREDTHS (1/100).
Third digit = THOUSANDTHS (1/1000).
To divide by 10, move the decimal point ONE place LEFT. By 100, TWO places. By 1000, THREE places.
teach.example: 0.7 means 7 tenths, or 7/10. 0.25 means 25 hundredths, or 25/100 = 1/4.

workedExample.question: What is 30,785 ÷ 1,000?
steps: Dividing by 1,000 means moving the decimal point THREE places LEFT.
30,785 has the decimal point at the end: 30,785. (or 30,785.0).

practice.question: Which is the largest?
practice.workingOut: Compare tenths: 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.4. First three tied at 5 tenths; then hundredths: 0.45 has 5, 0.50 has 0, 0.54 has 4. Largest is 0.54.

practice.question: What is 0.75 as a fraction in simplest form?

teach.title: Percentages
teach.plain: Per cent means 'out of 100'. 50% means 50 out of every 100, which is a half.
rules: 10% of anything = divide by 10.
50% = a half = divide by 2.
25% = a quarter = divide by 4.
1% = divide by 100.
To find other percentages, build them from the easy ones.

practice.question: A coat costs £80, reduced by 25%. Sale price?
20 out of 80 pupils walk. What percentage?

strands.name: Measurement
strands.description: Money, time, length, weight and metric units.

teach.title: Money problems
teach.plain: Money uses pounds and pence. £1 = 100p. Write amounts with 2 decimal places (like £4.50, not £4.5).
rules: Line up the decimal points when you add or subtract money.
Always write money with 2 decimal places: £4.50, £0.05, £12.00.
To change pence to pounds, divide by 100 (move decimal 2 places left).
To change pounds to pence, multiply by 100.
teach.tryThis: If a pen is 45p and a rubber is 30p, how much for both?

workedExample.question: A bike costs £120. Jacob has saved £72.50. How much more does he need?
steps: Line up the decimal points: 120.00 minus 72.50.

practice.question: A £120 bike. Jacob saved £72.50. How much more needed?
2 pizzas cost £17. What is the cost of 5 pizzas?
Sam has £20. Buys 3 notebooks at £2.75 each. Change?
Tom has £2,500. Spends £875 on laptop, £345 on phone. How much left?
Mean of 4 amounts is £16. Three are £15, £9, £21. What is the 4th?
Emma has £450, saves £125, £150, £175 more. How much now?

teach.title: Time
teach.plain: Time uses base 60, not base 10! 60 minutes = 1 hour. The 24-hour clock goes 00:00 to 23:59.
rules: 60 seconds = 1 minute. 60 minutes = 1 hour. 24 hours = 1 day.
After midday, 1pm = 13:00, 2pm = 14:00... add 12 to pm times.
To find a time gap, count up to the next whole hour, then onwards.
Do NOT subtract 52 minutes from 25 minutes directly — borrow 60, not 100.
teach.tryThis: A film starts 14:45 and lasts 1 hour 35 minutes. What time does it end?

workedExample.question: From 08:52 to 17:23, how many minutes pass?

practice.question: From 08:52 to 17:23, how many minutes?
practice.workingOut: 8 min to 9:00, then 8 hours (480), then 23 min. Total: 511.

practice.question: A film starts 14:45, lasts 1hr 35min. End time?
Train leaves 09:35 arrives 11:12. Journey length?
practice.workingOut: 09:35 to 10:35 is 1 hour. 10:35 to 11:12 is 37 min. Total: 1hr 37min.

teach.title: Metric conversions
teach.plain: Metric units work in tens, hundreds and thousands — so converting means multiplying or dividing by 10, 100 or 1,000.
rules: To go from BIG to SMALL units (e.g. km to m), MULTIPLY.
To go from SMALL to BIG units (e.g. cm to m), DIVIDE.
teach.example: Convert 3.5 km to m: big to small, so × 1,000. 3.5 × 1,000 = 3,500 m.

workedExample.question: What is 3.75m - 68cm?
steps: Make the units the SAME first.

practice.question: A ribbon is 1.5 m long. Cut 40 cm off. How much left?

teach.title: Perimeter & area
teach.plain: Perimeter is the distance AROUND a shape. Area is the space INSIDE a shape.
rules: Perimeter: add up ALL the sides.
Area of a rectangle: length × width.
Perimeter is measured in cm, m, km (length units).
Area is measured in SQUARE units: cm², m².
teach.example: A rectangle 8 cm by 5 cm. Perimeter = 8+5+8+5 = 26 cm. Area = 8 × 5 = 40 cm².
teach.tryThis: Find the perimeter and area of a rectangle 10 cm × 4 cm.

workedExample.question: A rectangle is 12 cm long and 5 cm wide. Find its perimeter AND area.
steps: Perimeter = add all sides. A rectangle has two longs and two widths.

practice.question: A square has area 64 cm². Length of one side?
Perimeter of a regular pentagon with one side 7 cm?
practice.workingOut: Regular pentagon has 5 equal sides. 5 × 7 = 35 cm.

practice.question: A garden is 20m by 15m. What is its perimeter?
Area of a square with sides 9 cm?

strands.name: Algebra Basics
strands.description: Missing numbers, simple equations and sequences.

teach.title: Missing numbers
teach.plain: In a missing-number problem, you work backwards. Use the OPPOSITE operation to the one in the question.
rules: + and - are opposites. × and ÷ are opposites.
Always CHECK by putting your answer back into the question.

workedExample.question: Solve 4a = 16.
steps: We need a number that, multiplied by 4, gives 16.

practice.question: A number minus 3,456 equals 5,432. What is the number?
Sum of two numbers is 10,000. One is 4,567. The other?

teach.title: Simple equations
teach.plain: An equation is a maths sentence with an equals sign. Letters stand for unknown numbers.
rules: The letter is a number we don't know yet.
Whatever you do to one side, do to the other.
To find the letter, undo the operation with its opposite.
2a means 2 × a. 3y means 3 × y.
teach.example: y + 5 = 12. Take 5 from both sides: y = 7.

workedExample.question: Expand 3(2y - 5).
steps: '3(2y - 5)' means 3 lots of everything inside the bracket.

practice.question: What is the value of 2a + 3 when a = 5?

teach.title: Number sequences
teach.plain: A sequence is a list of numbers that follow a rule. Find the rule by looking at how the numbers change.
rules: Look at the difference between consecutive numbers.
If the gap is the same each time, the rule is ADD or SUBTRACT that number.
If the numbers double or triple, the rule is MULTIPLY.
Check the rule works for EVERY pair in the sequence.
teach.example: 3, 7, 11, 15, ... Gap is +4 each time. Next term: 15 + 4 = 19.

workedExample.question: What is the next number? 2, 6, 18, 54, ?
steps: Look at each gap: 2 to 6, 6 to 18, 18 to 54.
Is it adding? 6 - 2 = 4, 18 - 6 = 12, 54 - 18 = 36. The differences change, so NOT adding a constant.
